title: Using Sora Editor with TextMate 0.18.1
content:
Hello, welcome to this blog. Today, we’re going to use Sora Editor with TextMate in version 0.18.1 using Java.

Why this post?

Due to the open source code of Sora Editor’s demo application is all about in Kotlin, some of the developers may not figure out how to write and understand Kotlin codes. In view of this, I’m here to show you how to use Sora Editor with TextMate in version 0.18.1 using Java.

注意

However, with the rapid development of technology, there are translators and converters of programming languages nowadays. For instance, if you want to convert Kotlin code to Java code or vice versa, you can use Kodezi to duel with this for you.

小提示

Before you start, make sure you have prepared textmate themes and languages files. You may find them in TextMate or from the source code of demo application.

Load Default Themes

try {
	FileProviderRegistry.getInstance().addFileProvider(new AssetsFileResolver(getAssets()));
    
    // set the themes that you want them to be initialized
    String[] themes = {"quietlight", "darcula"};
    var themeRegistry = ThemeRegistry.getInstance();
    
    for (int j = 0; j < themes.length; ) {
    	String name = themes[j];
        var path = "textmate/" + name + ".json"; // e.g. it will get "textmate/quietlight.json" file from asset
        themeRegistry.loadTheme(new ThemeModel(IThemeSource.fromInputStream(FileProviderRegistry.getInstance().tryGetInputStream(path), path, null), name));
        j++;

    }
} catch (Exception e) {
	e.printStackTrace();
}

Load Default Languages

// load all languages from the grammar root base
GrammarRegistry.getInstance().loadGrammars("textmate/languages.json");

Load Default Languages Dynamically

If you don’t want to load languages statically, you can load them dynamically either. In this case, we load Java language as an example.

GrammarDefinition grammarDefinition = new GrammarDefinition() {
    @Override
    public String getName() {
        return "java";
    }

    @Override
    public String getLanguageConfiguration() {
        return "textmate/java/language-configuration.json";
    }

    @Override
    public String getScopeName() {
        return "source.java";
    }

    @Override
    public IGrammarSource getGrammar() {
        return new IGrammarSource() {
            @Override
            public String getFilePath() {
                return "textmate/java/syntaxes/java.tmLanguage.json";
            }

            @Override
            public Reader getReader() throws IOException {
                return null;
            }
        };
    }
};

GrammarRegistry.getInstance().loadGrammar(grammarDefinition);
GrammarRegistry.getInstance().loadLanguageAndLanguageConfiguration(grammarDefinition);

Ensure TextMate Themes

try {
    var editorColorScheme = binding.editor.getColorScheme();
    if (!(editorColorScheme instanceof TextMateColorScheme)) {
        editorColorScheme = TextMateColorScheme.create(ThemeRegistry.getInstance());
        binding.editor.setColorScheme(editorColorScheme);
    }
} catch (Exception e) {
    e.printStackTrace();
}

Apply Languages

TextMateLanguage language;
var editorLanguage = binding.editor.getEditorLanguage();
if (editorLanguage instanceof TextMateLanguage) {
	language = (TextMateLanguage) editorLanguage;
    language.updateLanguage("source.java");
} else {
    language = TextMateLanguage.create("source.java", true);
}
binding.editor.setEditorLanguage(language);

Apply Themes

Here, I will detect light theme and dark theme, the key is to use ThemeRegistry.getInstance().setTheme() to apply themes.

int mode = getResources().getConfiguration().uiMode & Configuration.UI_MODE_NIGHT_MASK;
switch (mode) {
    case Configuration.UI_MODE_NIGHT_YES: // dark mode
        ThemeRegistry.getInstance().setTheme("darcula");
        break;

    case Configuration.UI_MODE_NIGHT_NO: // light mode
        ThemeRegistry.getInstance().setTheme("quietlight");
        break;

    case Configuration.UI_MODE_NIGHT_UNDEFINED: // unknown mode the device is using

        break;
}

Full source code

Here’s the source code of MainActivity.java that you’re now able to copy and paste them to your project once you got all of the sources and resources on your hands.

implementation(platform("io.github.Rosemoe.sora-editor:bom:0.18.1"))
implementation("io.github.Rosemoe.sora-editor:editor")
implementation("io.github.Rosemoe.sora-editor:language-textmate")

Or, if you IDE doesn’t support bom importing.

implementation "io.github.Rosemoe.sora-editor:editor:0.18.1"
implementation "io.github.Rosemoe.sora-editor:language-textmate:0.18.1"

If there’s no quick fix of packages importing on your IDE, here are the packages that you might need.

import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

import android.content.res.Configuration;
import android.os.Bundle;

import com.bluewhaleyt.soraeditortest.databinding.ActivityMainBinding;

import org.eclipse.tm4e.core.registry.IThemeSource;

import io.github.rosemoe.sora.widget.CodeEditor;
import io.github.rosemoe.sora.langs.textmate.TextMateColorScheme;
import io.github.rosemoe.sora.langs.textmate.TextMateLanguage;
import io.github.rosemoe.sora.langs.textmate.registry.FileProviderRegistry;
import io.github.rosemoe.sora.langs.textmate.registry.GrammarRegistry;
import io.github.rosemoe.sora.langs.textmate.registry.ThemeRegistry;
import io.github.rosemoe.sora.langs.textmate.registry.model.ThemeModel;
import io.github.rosemoe.sora.langs.textmate.registry.provider.AssetsFileResolver;

You’re not required to import kind of org.eclipse.tm4e.core dependencies on your own, Sora Editor includes them by default.

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    xmlns:app="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res-auto"
    xmlns:tools="http://schemas.android.com/tools"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    tools:context=".MainActivity">

    <io.github.rosemoe.sora.widget.CodeEditor
        android:id="@+id/editor"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="match_parent" />

</LinearLayout>

public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    ActivityMainBinding binding;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        binding = ActivityMainBinding.inflate(getLayoutInflater());
        setContentView(binding.getRoot());

        loadDefaultThemes();
        loadDefaultLanguages();

        ensureTextmateTheme();

        applyThemes();
        applyLanguages();

    }

    private void loadDefaultThemes() {

        try {
            FileProviderRegistry.getInstance().addFileProvider(new AssetsFileResolver(getAssets()));

            String[] themes = {"quietlight", "darcula"};
            var themeRegistry = ThemeRegistry.getInstance();

            for (int j = 0; j < themes.length;) {
                String name = themes[j];
                var path = "textmate/" + name + ".json";
                themeRegistry.loadTheme(new ThemeModel(IThemeSource.fromInputStream(FileProviderRegistry.getInstance().tryGetInputStream(path), path, null), name));
                j++;

            }
        } catch (Exception e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }

    }

    public void loadDefaultLanguages() {
        GrammarRegistry.getInstance().loadGrammars("textmate/languages.json");
    }

    public void ensureTextmateTheme() {

        try {
            var editorColorScheme = binding.editor.getColorScheme();
            if (!(editorColorScheme instanceof TextMateColorScheme)) {
                editorColorScheme = TextMateColorScheme.create(ThemeRegistry.getInstance());
                binding.editor.setColorScheme(editorColorScheme);
            }
        } catch (Exception e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }

    }

    public void applyThemes() {

        int mode = getResources().getConfiguration().uiMode & Configuration.UI_MODE_NIGHT_MASK;
        switch (mode) {
        case Configuration.UI_MODE_NIGHT_YES:
            ThemeRegistry.getInstance().setTheme("darcula");
            break;

        case Configuration.UI_MODE_NIGHT_NO:
            ThemeRegistry.getInstance().setTheme("quietlight");
            break;
        }

    }

    public void applyLanguages() {

        ensureTextmateTheme();

        TextMateLanguage language;
        var editorLanguage = binding.editor.getEditorLanguage();
        if (editorLanguage instanceof TextMateLanguage) {
            language = (TextMateLanguage) editorLanguage;
            language.updateLanguage("source.java");
        } else {
            language = TextMateLanguage.create("source.java", true);
        }
        binding.editor.setEditorLanguage(language);

    }
}

That’s all. I hope this blog post would help you.
